title: The Moon Rabbit of Korea
summary: A kind rabbit offers itself as food to a beggar, only to be rewarded with immortality on the Moon.
full_text:
In the ancient forests of East Asia, where the mist clings to the emerald canopy and the spirits of the land whisper through the bamboo, there once lived three humble animals: a monkey, a fox, and a rabbit. Though different in nature, they were bound by a shared desire to cultivate a heart of pure virtue. They believed that true enlightenment could only be achieved through acts of selfless charity, and so they made a solemn pact: on the day of the full moon, they would seek out someone in need and give everything they had to help them.

As the moon rose, silver and heavy in the night sky, an old beggar appeared on the forest path. He was bent with age, his clothes tattered rags, his voice a thin rasp of exhaustion. "I am starving," he pleaded, his eyes hollow with hunger. "Please, if you have any food, spare a morsel for a dying man."

The monkey, eager to prove his kindness, leaped into the high branches of the fruit trees. He gathered the sweetest, ripest mangoes and berries, presenting them to the beggar with a flourish of pride. The fox, cunning but well-meaning, ventured to the nearby stream and caught a shimmering silver fish, offering it to the man with a respectful bow.

Then it was the rabbit's turn. The rabbit looked around the forest floor, but there were no fruits to be found, and he had no skill in fishing. He felt a surge of panic and shame. How could he possibly match the generosity of his friends? He searched until his paws were sore, but the forest offered nothing but dry grass and grey stones.

As the beggar began to fade, the rabbit realized that the only thing he possessed of any value was his own life. With a heart full of resolve and a terrifying courage, the rabbit gathered dry branches and built a small, crackling fire. He looked at the beggar one last time, whispered a prayer of peace, and without hesitation, threw himself into the flames.

But as the fire roared, a miracle occurred. The beggar was no ordinary man; he was Śakra, the Great King of Heaven, who had descended to the mortal realm in disguise to test the purity of the animals' hearts. He had seen the monkey's pride and the fox's habit, but in the rabbit, he had found a spirit of absolute, unconditional sacrifice.

With a wave of his hand, Śakra extinguished the flames. The rabbit was not consumed by the fire; instead, he was healed and bathed in a celestial light. "Your heart is a mirror of the divine," Śakra declared. "Such generosity cannot be forgotten by the universe."

To ensure that the rabbit's virtue would be a beacon for all eternity, Śakra lifted him from the earth and placed him upon the face of the Moon. There, in the silver silence of the lunar plains, the rabbit was given a sacred task: to pound the ingredients of the elixir of immortality in a great mortar and pestle.

Now, when the full moon hangs low in the sky, if you look closely at the dark markings upon its surface, you can see the silhouette of Daltokki, the Moon Rabbit. He remains there still, a timeless reminder that true generosity is not measured by what we have, but by what we are willing to give. In Korea, during the festival of Chuseok, families look up at the moon and tell the story of the rabbit, teaching their children that the highest form of love is the one that asks for nothing in return.

title: Bai Ze: The Beast of Infinite Knowledge
summary: A mythical beast appears to the Yellow Emperor, revealing all knowledge of spirits and monsters in the world.
full_text:
In the misty mountains of ancient China, where the bamboo grows so tall it seems to touch the clouds and the mist hangs in the valleys like silk curtains, there lived a creature of impossible wisdom. He was called Bai Ze — the White Marsh — a beast with the body of a lion and the head of a human, covered in white fur that glowed like moonlight.

Bai Ze could speak every language, including the languages of animals, trees, and stones. He knew the names of every spirit, demon, and god. He understood the past, present, and future as a single, continuous tapestry. But he never spoke unless spoken to, and he never offered wisdom that was not asked for.

One day, the Yellow Emperor, Huangdi, was hunting in the mountains. He had conquered the warring tribes, unified China, and established the first civilization. But he was troubled. Strange events were happening across his kingdom — crops failing, rivers flooding, children born with marks that foretold doom. The emperor suspected that malevolent spirits were at work, but he did not know their names, and without their names, he could not banish them.

In the deepest part of the forest, the emperor encountered Bai Ze. The beast sat on a moss-covered stone, his white fur gleaming in the dim light. "You seek the names of the spirits," Bai Ze said, without being asked.

The emperor was amazed. "Yes," he said. "Tell me their names, and I will protect my people."

Bai Ze spoke for three days and three nights, reciting the names and natures of 11,520 spirits — those that caused disease, drought, madness, and misfortune. For each spirit, he gave the method of exorcism. The emperor recorded every word.

This was the Bai Ze Tu, the Illustrated Treatise of Bai Ze — the first encyclopedia of supernatural creatures in China. It was passed down through generations of Taoist priests and is still used today in Chinese folk religion.
moral: Knowledge dispels darkness—know your enemies and you can defeat them.
source: Classic Chinese texts (Book of Song references)

title: Hanako-San: The Bathroom Ghost
summary: A ghost girl haunts school bathrooms in Japan. Knock three times and ask if she is there—if she answers, you are marked.
full_text:
In the sterile, echoing hallways of Japanese schools, where the scent of floor wax and old chalk lingers in the air, there is a legend spoken only in hurried whispers. It is a story that turns the mundane act of visiting the restroom into a ritual of terror. This is the legend of Hanako-san, the ghost of the girl in the third stall.

The ritual is precise, for the spirits of the school do not tolerate mistakes. To summon her, one must travel to the third floor at the stroke of midnight, when the school is a skeletal remains of its daytime self. You must enter the girls' bathroom, walk past the first and second stalls, and stop before the third. There, you must knock three times on the wooden door—three slow, deliberate thuds that reverberate through the tiled silence. Then, in a voice barely above a whisper, you must ask: "Hanako-san, are you there?"

For most, the answer is silence, and the silence is a mercy. But for some, a voice answers. It is a voice that sounds like a child's, yet carries the weight of a thousand years of sorrow. "Yes," she whispers. "I am here."

At that moment, the air in the bathroom turns freezing, and the lights flicker with an unstable, rhythmic pulse. Some claim to see a pale, small hand emerge from the gap in the stall door, its skin the color of wet ash. Others report seeing a young girl with a sharp, bobbed haircut and a vibrant red skirt, her eyes wide and vacant, staring from a place beyond the veil of the living.

The origins of Hanako-san are as varied as the schools she haunts. In some versions, she was a victim of the Great Air Raids of World War II, a terrified child who hid in the third stall of her school bathroom, only for the building to collapse around her in a rain of fire. In others, she was a student who suffered the cruelties of bullying, her spirit broken by the laughter of her peers until she sought the finality of suicide in the very place she felt most isolated. Some even whisper that she was murdered by a parent or a stranger, her life stolen in a moment of senseless violence.

Regardless of how she arrived, Hanako-san is now a fixture of the urban landscape. She is the manifestation of childhood fear, the embodiment of the secrets we hide in the locked rooms of our lives. In Tokyo, a shrine has even been erected to honor her, where children leave candy and notes, hoping to appease the spirit of the girl who never got to grow up.

But the legend persists. Every year, a new generation of students dares their friends to knock three times and call her name. They do it for the thrill, for the social currency of bravery, unaware that once you call to Hanako-san, you have opened a door that cannot always be closed. For in the silence of the third stall, Hanako-san is always listening, waiting for the next curious soul to ask if she is still there.
moral: Fear lives in the places we avoid—the locked room, the dark.

title: Teke Teke: The Railway Ghost
summary: A woman who died on the railway returns as a vengeful spirit, her body sliced in half, hunting the living.
full_text:
The railway lines of Japan are veins of steel and electricity that pulse with the frantic energy of millions. But in the dead of night, when the last train has departed and the stations are empty, these tracks become the hunting grounds of a nightmare. They call her Teke Teke, a name derived not from a word, but from the sound of her movement—the rhythmic, scraping noise of elbows hitting concrete.

Teke Teke was once a schoolgirl, full of dreams and laughter, until a singular, horrific accident tore her world apart. The stories vary—some say she fell by mistake, others say she was pushed—but the result was the same. A speeding train sliced through her body, severing her at the waist. She did not die instantly; she lay on the cold gravel of the tracks, screaming for help, while the station staff, paralyzed by shock or indifference, failed to save her in time. In her final moments, her terror morphed into a concentrated, obsidian hatred. She died not as a girl, but as an Onryō—a vengeful spirit bound to the place of her mutilation.

Now, she roams the urban periphery, a creature of halves. She has no legs, only a torso that she drags behind her with a strength that defies physics. As she moves, her elbows strike the ground: *teke, teke, teke*. To the unsuspecting ear, it sounds like a distant, rhythmic tapping, almost benign. But if you hear that sound when you are alone at night, you have already entered her orbit.

Teke Teke is a relentless predator. She does not tire, and she does not stop. If she spots a victim, she accelerates with terrifying speed, her upper body gliding across the pavement like a blade. Those who try to outrun her find that she is always faster, the *teke-teke* sound growing louder and more frantic until she is right behind them. Her primary weapon is a great, rusted scythe, or sometimes her own jagged nails, which she uses to slice her victims in half at the waist. She does not kill for hunger, but for symmetry; she ensures that her victims share her own grotesque disfigurement.

There is a darker layer to the legend: the story of Kashima Reiko. Some say Teke Teke is a manifestation of Kashima, a spirit who haunts the bathrooms of schools, asking those she encounters, "Where are my legs?" If the answer is wrong, the result is the same: a sudden, violent bisection.

The legend of Teke Teke serves as a grim reminder of the fragility of life and the enduring power of resentment. It warns against the indifference of strangers and the permanence of loss. In the quiet hours of the night, when the wind howls through the railway gantries, the people of Japan remember to walk quickly and never look back. For in the shadows of the station, something is scraping the ground, searching for someone to join her in her half-lived existence.
moral: Violent death leaves souls unable to rest in peace.

title: Kintaro: The Golden Boy of Mountains
summary: A supernaturally strong child raised by animals in the mountains becomes a legendary hero.
full_text:
In the emerald heart of Mount Ashigara, where the waterfalls roar with a primordial intensity and the ancient cedars touch the clouds, there lived a child known as Kintarō—the Golden Boy. Kintarō was not like other children. He was born with a strength that defied the laws of nature and a spirit as bright and unyielding as the sun. Some say he was the son of a noble samurai who had been forced into exile, while others whisper that he was a gift from the mountains themselves, born from the union of a human and a divine spirit.

Kintarō was raised by a yama-uba, a mountain witch of formidable power and a heart of hidden gold. She taught him the secrets of the forest, the language of the winds, and the rhythms of the earth. Clad in a simple red haragake apron with the golden kanji for "gold" emblazoned upon its chest, and wielding a heavy iron hatchet, Kintarō spent his days in a state of joyous, violent play.

His companions were not children, but the wild beasts of the mountain. He would wrestle bears into submission, pinning the massive creatures with a laugh that echoed through the valleys. He would race with the deer and outrun the swiftest hawks. The animals did not fear him; they respected him as their peer, for Kintarō possessed a purity of heart that resonated with the wild. He was the protector of the mountain, a golden spark of energy who helped the local woodcutters fell the most stubborn trees and cleared the paths of fallen boulders with a single, thunderous blow.

As the years passed, the legend of the Golden Boy spread beyond the peaks of Ashigara. Word reached the ears of Minamoto no Yorimitsu, a great samurai and a master of the martial arts. Yorimitsu, seeking warriors of exceptional caliber for his retinue, traveled to the mountains to find the boy. He found Kintarō in the middle of a sumo match with a giant bear, the earth trembling beneath their weight. Impressed by the boy's raw power and his innate nobility, Yorimitsu offered him a place by his side in the capital of Kyoto.

Kintarō accepted, trading his forest solitude for the discipline of the sword and the complexities of the court. He took the name Sakata no Kintoki and became the chief of Yorimitsu's "Four Braves." No longer just a wild child of the woods, he became a paragon of the samurai spirit—combining his superhuman strength with a refined mastery of combat. His most famous feat was the defeat of Shuten-dōji, the terrifying demon king of Mount Ōe, a battle that required both the strength of a bear and the cunning of a man.

Kintarō's legacy endures in Japan as a symbol of healthy growth, courage, and the untamed potential of childhood. On Children's Day, families still display Kintarō dolls, hoping their own sons will inherit the Golden Boy's strength and his unwavering, pure heart. He remains a reminder that true power is not found in the absence of kindness, but in the ability to protect others with a strength born of love and nature.
moral: True strength comes from a pure heart, not from weapons.

title: Dangun: The Founder of Korea
summary: A divine being descends from heaven and marries a bear to found the first Korean kingdom.
full_text:
Before the first cities were built, before the first scrolls were written, there was a time of profound transition in the lands of the East. In the celestial realms, the Lord of Heaven, Hwanin, looked down upon the world with a mixture of compassion and curiosity. His son, Hwanung, shared this longing. While the heavens were a place of eternal peace, Hwanung craved the tactile reality of the earth—the scent of rain on dry soil, the whisper of the wind through the pines, and the struggle and triumph of mortal existence.

Moved by his son's sincerity, Hwanin granted Hwanung permission to descend. Hwanung did not come alone; he brought with him three thousand followers and the divine ministers of wind, rain, and clouds. They descended upon the sacred peak of Taebaeksan, landing beneath a divine birch tree, the shindansu, where Hwanung established Sinsi, the City of God. From this celestial hub, Hwanung began the great work of civilizing the land. He taught the people the art of agriculture, the secrets of medicine, and the laws of morality, fostering a society based on harmony and mutual respect.

However, Hwanung's presence attracted the attention of the wild. A bear and a tiger, both driven by a desperate yearning to escape their animal nature and ascend to humanity, approached Hwanung with a plea. "We wish to be human," they begged. "We wish to live under your laws and share in your wisdom."

Hwanung, testing their resolve, gave them a challenge that seemed impossible. He handed each of them twenty cloves of garlic and a bundle of sacred mugwort. "You must retreat into a dark cave," he commanded. "You must eat only this food and remain out of the reach of the sun's rays for one hundred days. If you can endure the darkness and the hunger, you shall be transformed."

The tiger, impulsive and restless, found the silence of the cave suffocating. After only a few weeks, the craving for the hunt and the lure of the forest became unbearable. With a roar of frustration, the tiger abandoned the trial and vanished back into the wild, forever remaining a beast.

But the bear persevered. She endured the biting cold, the oppressive darkness, and the bitter taste of the garlic. She meditated on her desire for humanity, transforming her patience into a spiritual force. On the hundredth day, as a sliver of light pierced the cave's entrance, the bear's form shifted. Her fur vanished, her claws softened, and she emerged as a woman of extraordinary beauty and grace, known as Ungnyeo.

Ungnyeo's heart was filled with gratitude, but she was lonely in her new humanity. She prayed beneath the divine birch tree, asking for a companion to share her life. Hwanung, moved by her devotion, took her as his wife. Their union, a bridge between the divine and the earthly, produced a son named Tan'gun Wanggŏm.

Tan'gun grew to be a leader of unmatched wisdom and authority. He founded Gojoseon, the first Korean kingdom, establishing its capital at Asadal. He ruled not through fear, but through the philosophy of "Hongik Ingan"—the principle of broadly benefiting all humanity. Under his guidance, the people learned to live in balance with the heavens and the earth. To this day, the founding of Gojoseon remains the cornerstone of Korean identity, a story of patience, transformation, and the divine spark that ignited a civilization.

title: Kappa: The River imp
summary: A water spirit that lives in rivers, respects honor, and can be fooled by a simple formality.
full_text:
In the crystalline streams and hidden ponds of Japan, there lives a creature of mischief and honor known as the Kappa. Part turtle, part monkey, and entirely unpredictable, the Kappa is a water imp whose existence is woven into the very fabric of the Japanese countryside.

The most defining feature of a Kappa is the shallow dish, or sara, located on top of its head. This dish is perpetually filled with water, and it is the source of the Kappa's superhuman strength. If the water spills, the Kappa becomes weak and docile, losing its power to harm or manipulate. This biological quirk is the key to surviving an encounter with these river spirits.

Kappas are notorious pranksters. They are known to pull unsuspecting swimmers beneath the surface, steal crops from the riverbanks, and frighten travelers with sudden, splashing appearances. But they have a peculiar obsession: they love cucumbers more than anything else in the world. This is why, even today, cucumber maki rolls are called "kappa maki"—a nod to the river imp's favorite snack.

Yet, despite their malice, Kappas are bound by a rigid code of honor. They are meticulously polite and deeply respectful of social formalities. If a human encounters a Kappa and bows deeply, the Kappa, compelled by its own nature, must bow back. In that single, reflexive movement, the water in the sara spills, and the Kappa is rendered helpless.

There is a famous legend of a nobleman who was crossing a bridge when a Kappa attempted to drag him into the river. Thinking quickly, the nobleman bowed with exaggerated formality. The Kappa, unable to resist the urge to reciprocate, bowed back, spilling its life-force into the dirt. Instead of killing the weakened spirit, the nobleman struck a bargain: the Kappa would teach him the secrets of medicine and bone-setting in exchange for its life.

The Kappa agreed, and for years, the nobleman used this secret knowledge to heal the sick and injured of his province. The story of the Kappa teaches a vital lesson: that strength is not always found in force, but in the understanding of one's opponent's nature. It is a reminder that respect and formality can be the most powerful weapons of all, and that even the most dangerous monster can be tamed by a simple act of politeness.
moral: Formality and respect can defeat any opponent.

title: Gashadokuro: The Giant Skull
summary: A massive skeleton fifteen times bigger than a human appears at night, searching for living people to bite.
full_text:
In the quiet corners of Japan, where the wind whistles through abandoned shrines and the moonlight casts long, skeletal shadows, there is a legend that emerges whenever the earth is stained by mass tragedy. It is the legend of the Gashadokuro—the Starving Skeleton.

The Gashadokuro are not born of a single soul, but are forged from the collective agony of hundreds of people who died in war or famine and were left unburied. Their bones, bleached white by the sun and hardened by hatred, merge together to form a single, colossal skeleton, fifteen times the size of a human. They are the manifestations of the "unmourned," spirits who have been denied the peace of a proper burial and are now driven by an insatiable, eternal hunger.

The Gashadokuro roam the countryside at night, their footsteps sounding like distant thunder. They are heralded by the faint, rhythmic ringing of bells—bells taken from the graves they left behind. This sound is a warning, a signal to any traveler that the giant is near.

The Gashadokuro does not hunt with a plan; it hunts by instinct. It searches for living people to bite, hoping that by consuming the life-force of the living, it can finally fill the void in its chest. When it finds a victim, it reaches down with massive, bony fingers and snatches them from the earth, crushing them in a single, indifferent motion.

But there is a way to survive an encounter with the giant. The Gashadokuro is a creature of pattern and expectation. If you hear the bells, you must not run, for the giant can track the scent of fear across miles of terrain. Instead, you must make yourself small. Pull your hat low, tuck your thumb into your fist, and hold your breath. The giant is looking for "big prey," and if you can successfully mimic the stillness of a stone, the Gashadokuro may simply step over you, continuing its endless search.

The legend of the Gashadokuro is a haunting meditation on the importance of honor and memory. It teaches that the dead do not simply vanish; they remain as a reflection of how they were treated in life. The giant skeleton is a reminder that a society that forgets its fallen, or ignores the suffering of the masses, creates its own monsters. To bury the dead with dignity is not just a gesture of respect, but a necessary act of protection for the living.

title: Kitsune: The Fox Spirits
summary: Intelligent foxes with magical powers—they can shapeshift, possess humans, and serve as messengers of the gods.
full_text:
In the mist-shrouded forests and ancient shrines of Japan, there exists a creature of profound intelligence and mercurial nature: the Kitsune. While to the untrained eye they appear as simple foxes, the Kitsune are in truth powerful yōkai, spirits of the wild whose wisdom grows with every passing century. Their most celebrated gift is the art of bakeru—the ability to shift their form and weave illusions so potent that they can deceive even the most discerning human mind.

As a Kitsune ages, its power expands, and its physical form evolves to reflect its growing mastery over the spiritual realm. The most coveted mark of this progression is the growth of additional tails. A young fox may have only one, but as it reaches a thousand years of age, it may grow up to nine. The Nine-Tailed Fox is a being of near-godlike power, its fur turning a shimmering gold or a pristine white, its eyes reflecting the secrets of the cosmos. Such a creature possesses the ability to see and hear everything that happens across the world, moving between dimensions with a mere flicker of its tail.

Within the hierarchy of these spirits, there is a stark divide. The Zenko, or celestial foxes, are the benevolent servants of Inari Ōkami, the deity of rice and prosperity. These white-furred messengers are guardians of the harvest and bringers of good fortune. They are often depicted as serene statues flanking the vermillion gates of Inari shrines, where they are honored with offerings of abura-age—deep-fried tofu—their favorite delicacy. To earn the favor of a Zenko is to invite abundance into one's home, but to disrespect them is to invite a misfortune that can last for generations.

In contrast are the Yako, the wild foxes. These are the tricksters, the mischief-makers who delight in the confusion of mortals. A Yako does not seek to protect; it seeks to play. They are the architects of the 'fox-fire' (kitsunebi), floating orbs of ghostly light that lead weary travelers deep into the mountains, far from the path, until they are hopelessly lost in a labyrinth of trees and fog. Their favorite game is the deception of the senses: they may transform into a beautiful woman to seduce a lonely man, or a respected monk to lead a village astray, only to reveal their true, vulpine nature at the moment of greatest impact—often leaving their victim to wake up in a manure pit, clutching a handful of leaves they believed to be gold.

Yet, the Kitsune are not merely agents of chaos. There is a deep, enduring theme of loyalty in their lore. The 'Fox Wife' is a recurring figure in Japanese legend—a Kitsune who, moved by the kindness of a human man, assumes human form to marry him and bear his children. These unions are often filled with tenderness and devotion, lasting for years until the secret is accidentally revealed—perhaps by a small child seeing a tail flick beneath a kimono, or a dog barking at the invisible spirit. Even in the heartbreak of their forced departure, the Kitsune often leaves behind a legacy of love and a lesson about the ephemeral nature of truth.

To live in the shadow of the Kitsune is to accept that the world is not always as it seems. Whether they are guiding a farmer to a bountiful harvest or leading a proud nobleman into a small, muddy pond, the foxes remind us that intelligence is a weapon, and that the line between the divine and the mischievous is as thin as a single strand of fox-fur.

title: The Legend of the Bamboo Cutter
summary: In the ancient days of Japan, there lived an old bamboo cutter named Sanuki.. He was a man of simple means and a gentle heart, spending his days in the emerald depths of the bamboo groves that whispered secrets in the wind.
full_text:
In the ancient days of Japan, there lived an old bamboo cutter named Sanuki. He was a man of simple means and a gentle heart, spending his days in the emerald depths of the bamboo groves that whispered secrets in the wind. One evening, as the sun dipped below the horizon, painting the sky in hues of violet and gold, Sanuki spotted a peculiar sight: a single stalk of bamboo glowing with an ethereal, golden light. Drawn by a force he could not explain, he approached the stalk and, with a cautious blade, sliced it open. To his utter astonishment, nestled within the hollow stem was a tiny girl, no larger than his thumb, radiating a soft, celestial luminescence. 

Sanuki and his wife, who had long yearned for a child, took the infant into their home with a devotion that bordered on the sacred. They named her Kaguya-hime, the Radiant Princess. As the months passed, a miracle unfolded. Kaguya did not grow as human children do; instead, she blossomed with an unnatural speed and a grace that defied earthly description. Within a mere three months, she had transformed into a young woman of such breathtaking beauty that her presence seemed to illuminate the very air around her. Her skin was like polished ivory, her eyes held the depth of the midnight sky, and her voice was as melodic as the mountain streams.

News of the Radiant Princess spread like wildfire across the provinces, reaching the ears of the most powerful men in the land. Princes, lords, and nobles flocked to Sanuki's humble cottage, each desperate to claim Kaguya's hand in marriage. Kaguya, however, felt no attraction to these earthly suitors. She possessed a wisdom and a melancholy that seemed to come from another world. To discourage them, she set four impossible tasks: the suitors were to bring her the Stone Bowl of Buddha, a jeweled branch from the legendary Mount Horai, the robe of the fire-rat, and a brilliant jewel from a dragon's neck.

One by one, the suitors failed. Some attempted to deceive her with clever fakes, only for Kaguya to expose their lies with a single, piercing glance. Others ventured into the wild, losing their lives to the perils of the quest. Even the Emperor of Japan, captivated by her beauty, sought her hand. While he succeeded in bringing her the robe, Kaguya's heart remained distant. She would often spend her nights gazing up at the full moon, her eyes filling with tears of an inexplicable longing. 

'My father and mother are not of this earth,' she finally confessed to the old bamboo cutter, her voice trembling with sorrow. 'I am a daughter of the Moon, sent here for a time to learn the ways of humanity. But the time of my exile has come to an end. The celestial host is coming to reclaim me.'

On the night of the autumn full moon, as the silver orb reached its zenith, a shimmering carriage descended from the heavens, pulled by celestial beings who glowed with a light that eclipsed the stars. The Emperor's guards tried to protect Kaguya, but the heavenly host moved with a power that rendered all human effort futile. As the angels surrounded her, Kaguya wept, embracing the old bamboo cutter and his wife one last time, thanking them for the love and kindness they had shown her. 

Before she ascended, she left behind a robe of celestial feathers and a letter for the Emperor, expressing her gratitude but confirming that her place was among the lunar spirits. As she donned the robe, her earthly memories and sorrows faded, replaced by the serene indifference of the moon. She ascended into the sky, leaving the world below in a state of profound grief. The Emperor, heartbroken, ordered his men to burn the robe and the letter on the highest mountain peak, hoping the smoke would reach the Moon and carry his longing to the princess. Hence the name Mount Fuji, the mountain of immortality, where the smoke of a broken heart once rose to the stars.
source: The Tale of the Bamboo Cutter (Taketori Monogatari)

title: Orochi the Eight-Headed Serpent
summary: The storm god Susanoo-no-Mikoto had been banished from Takamagahara, the Plain of High Heaven, after a furious quarrel with his sister Amaterasu, the sun goddess.
full_text:
The storm god Susanoo-no-Mikoto had been banished from Takamagahara, the Plain of High Heaven, after a furious quarrel with his sister Amaterasu, the sun goddess. He descended to the land of Izumo, following the Hi River upstream, where he saw chopsticks floating on the current. Guessing that people lived nearby, he walked until he found an elderly couple weeping beside a young girl.

The old man introduced himself as Ashinazuchi, his wife as Tenazuchi. They had once had eight daughters, but every year a terrible monster came from the mountains of Koshi — Yamata no Orochi, the eight-headed and eight-tailed serpent. Its eyes were red as winter cherries, moss and trees grew on its back, and its body spanned eight valleys and eight hills. Seven daughters had been devoured. Only Kushinadahime remained, and the serpent was due to claim her at any moment.

Susanoo looked upon Kushinadahime and was moved by her beauty and the family's despair. "Give her to me as my wife," he said, "and I will destroy the serpent." The couple agreed at once.

Susanoo transformed Kushinadahime into a comb and placed it in his hair for safekeeping. Then he instructed the couple to brew eight vats of the strongest sake — eight-fold refined rice wine — and to build a fence with eight gates, placing a vat at each gate. When the sake was ready, they hid and waited.

At dusk, Yamata no Orochi arrived. Each of its eight heads plunged into a separate vat and drank deeply. The powerful sake soon took effect: the monster grew drowsy, its eight heads drooped, and it fell into a drunken sleep.

Susanoo drew his sword, the Totsuka-no-Tsurugi, and began cutting. He hacked through each of the eight heads, each of the eight tails, until the Hi River ran red with blood. But when his blade struck the fourth tail, it chipped against something hard. Curious, Susanoo split the tail open and found a magnificent sword concealed within the serpent's flesh — the Ame-no-Murakumo-no-Tsurugi, the Sword of the Gathering Clouds of Heaven, later known as Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi, the Grass-Cutting Sword.

Recognizing its divine power, Susanoo presented the sword to his sister Amaterasu as a gift of reconciliation. It became one of the Three Imperial Regalia of Japan, symbols of the emperor's divine right to rule. As for Susanoo, he married Kushinadahime and built a palace at Suga in Izumo, where he composed the first waka poem in Japanese literary tradition — a song about clouds rising over his new home.

title: Amaterasu and the Cave
summary: Amaterasu, the Sun Goddess of Shinto, is the most important deity in the Japanese pantheon.
full_text:
Amaterasu, the Sun Goddess of Shinto, is the most important deity in the Japanese pantheon. Her name means "Shining in Heaven," and she is said to be the direct ancestor of the Japanese imperial family. But even a sun goddess has her moments of darkness.

Amaterasu lived in the celestial palace of Takamagahara with her siblings: Tsukuyomi, the moon god, and Susanoo, the storm god. Susanoo was wild and unpredictable, a force of chaos in the orderly heavens. He destroyed Amaterasu's rice fields, defiled her weaving hall, and threw a flayed horse through the roof of her sacred chamber.

Amaterasu was heartbroken and furious. She retreated into the Heavenly Rock Cave, called the Ama-no-Iwato. She sealed the entrance with a massive boulder. With the sun goddess hidden, the world fell into perpetual darkness. Crops withered, the gods stumbled blindly, and evil spirits roamed freely.

The eight million gods of Shinto gathered outside the cave. They tried every trick to lure Amaterasu out. They offered her jewels, food, and prayers. Nothing worked. Finally, the goddess Ame-no-Uzume, the spirit of revelry, devised a plan. She turned over a tub, climbed on top, and began a wild, lewd dance. The other gods laughed and cheered. The noise was so great that Amaterasu grew curious.

"What is happening?" she asked from inside the cave.

"We have found a goddess more beautiful than you," Ame-no-Uzume replied. "Come and see."

Jealous and intrigued, Amaterasu cracked the boulder open a sliver. In that instant, the god Ame-no-Tajikarao threw the boulder aside, and the goddess Ishikoridome held up a mirror. Amaterasu saw her own reflection for the first time. She was so dazzled by her own beauty that she stepped forward — and the world was flooded with light once more.

Amaterasu's return from the cave is celebrated every year in Shinto rituals. The mirror she saw is preserved at the Grand Shrine of Ise, the holiest site in Japan. The story is a reminder that even the sun can be obscured by sorrow, but light always returns — and sometimes, laughter is the force that brings it back.

title: Tao and the Mountain
summary: In ancient times, during the Warring States period, two great mountains stood between the southern part of Jizhou and the northern bank of the Yellow River.
full_text: In ancient times, during the Warring States period, two great mountains stood between the southern part of Jizhou and the northern bank of the Yellow River. The first was called Taihang, and the second was called Wangwu. They were not ordinary hills; they were vast, towering masses of rock and forest that rose like walls between the provinces, forcing travelers to walk hundreds of li around them to reach the other side. At the northern foot of these mountains lived an old man named Yu Gong, which meant Foolish Old Man. He was nearly ninety years old, his beard white as snow and his back bent like a bow, but his eyes still held the stubborn light of a man who had never accepted defeat. The mountains blocked his family's path to the south. Every time they needed to visit the market or see relatives, they had to make a journey of many days around the base of the peaks. "This is intolerable," Yu Gong announced one morning, as he watched his sons pack provisions for yet another long detour. "I am going to remove these mountains." His wife laughed. "You cannot even move a pebble," she said. "What makes you think you can level two mountains? And where would you put the earth and the rocks?" "We will carry them to the edge of the Bohai Sea," Yu Gong replied. "The sea is vast. It will not refuse a few basketfuls of dirt." His sons, though skeptical, loved their father and agreed to help. Even a young widow's child of seven or eight, who lived nearby, came along to lend his small hands. The next morning, they began. Armed with picks, shovels, and bamboo baskets, Yu Gong and his family climbed the slopes of Taihang Mountain and began to chip away at its ancient stone. They filled their baskets with earth and rock, carried them down to the shore of the Bohai Sea, dumped them into the water, and then walked all the way back to the mountain. The journey took them one full year, there and back. A wise old man named Zhi Sou, whose name meant Clever Old Man, came to watch them. He stood on a ridge with his hands on his hips and laughed until his sides ached. "You are the biggest fool I have ever seen," he called down to Yu Gong. "You are ninety years old, weak as a winter fly, and you cannot even bend a blade of grass. How do you expect to move these great mountains?" Yu Gong straightened his back with difficulty and looked up at the mocking man. "You are more stubborn than a rock," he said, "and less wise than the child who helps me. Listen to me, Clever Old Man. When I die, I have sons. When my sons die, they have grandsons. My grandsons will have sons of their own. Generation after generation, there is no end to my descendants. But these mountains will not grow any taller. Every basket I remove makes them smaller. So tell me, why can I not move them?" Zhi Sou had no answer. He shook his head and walked away, muttering about fools and wasted effort. Yu Gong and his family continued their work. They chipped and carried, day after day, year after year. The mountain god, who lived deep in the heart of Taihang, grew alarmed. This foolish old man was actually making progress. At this rate, in a few thousand years, the entire mountain might be gone. The god reported the matter to the Emperor of Heaven. The Emperor looked down from his celestial palace and saw the old man, tiny as an ant, chipping away at the vast stone face of the mountain. He saw the patient rhythm of the picks, the steady march of the basket carriers, the unyielding determination in Yu Gong's ancient eyes. The Emperor was moved. "Such sincerity cannot be ignored," he said. He summoned the two sons of the god Kua'e, giants of immense strength, and commanded them to descend to earth. That night, while Yu Gong and his family slept, the two giants lifted Taihang Mountain and Wangwu Mountain onto their shoulders and carried them away. One mountain was placed in the far east of Shu, and the other was set down in the far north of Yong. From that day forward, the road from Jizhou to the southern bank of the Hanshui River was clear and level. Yu Gong woke the next morning to find an open plain where the mountains had stood. He smiled, said a quiet prayer of thanks, and went back to his work, for there was always something that needed doing.
moral: Self-sacrifice for others is the highest virtue.

title: Mulan
summary: The ballad of Mulan was first sung in the northern dynasties of China, during a time when the Toba people ruled the north and the Rouran tribes pressed against the borders like a relentless tide.
full_text: The ballad of Mulan was first sung in the northern dynasties of China, during a time when the Toba people ruled the north and the Rouran tribes pressed against the borders like a relentless tide. It was an age of conscription, when the Khan's call to arms reached every village, every hamlet, every isolated farmstead where a man of fighting age might be found. In a small village in what is now Henan province, an old soldier named Hua sat in the doorway of his home, watching the evening light fade over the mulberry trees. His back was bent from years of campaigning, his joints swollen with age, and his breath came short after even the slightest exertion. He had given his youth to the emperor, and now the emperor was asking for the rest of him. The conscription notice arrived at dusk, carried by a mounted messenger whose horse was lathered with sweat. Hua's daughter Mulan took the scroll from the messenger's hand, her fingers trembling as she unrolled it. The characters were stark and unforgiving: the Khan was raising a great army, and every household must send a man. Twelve scrolls of conscription orders, and on every scroll, her father's name appeared. Mulan was twenty-four years old, unmarried, and skilled with a sword and bow. Her father had taught her himself, when she was a child, sitting beside him as he mended his old weapons and told her stories of the campaigns he had fought. She had learned archery by shooting at targets shaped from straw, and swordsmanship by practicing the forms until her arms ached. It had been a game then, a way to spend time with her father. Now it was the only hope her family had. "Father cannot go," she told her mother that night, as they sat by the dim light of a cooking fire. "He will die on the road before he ever reaches the battlefield." Her mother wept. "But there is no son," she said. "Your younger brother is barely a child. If your father does not go, the soldiers will come for him, and they will not be gentle." Mulan looked at her mother with eyes that had already made their decision. "I will go in his place." The next morning, she went to the market and bought a horse from the east market, a saddle and blanket from the west, a bridle from the south, and a long whip from the north. She cut her hair short, bound her chest with linen strips, and dressed herself in her father's old armor. It was rusty and ill-fitting, but it would serve. When she mounted the horse and looked back at her weeping parents, she did not allow herself to cry. She rode away from everything she had ever known. For twelve years, Mulan fought in the Khan's armies. She crossed ten thousand miles of hostile territory, climbing mountains so steep that the horses slipped on loose scree, fording rivers so cold that men died of exposure on the opposite bank. She slept in her armor, ate stale millet cakes, and learned to read the terrain for ambushes. She killed men in close combat and watched friends die beside her. Through it all, she kept her secret. She never undressed completely, never bathed with the other soldiers, never allowed anyone to see the truth beneath the steel and leather. The other warriors respected her for her skill and her silence. "Brother Hua is fierce," they said, "but strange. He keeps to himself." When the war finally ended, the Khan held a great audience in the Hall of Brilliance. He rewarded his soldiers with promotions and gold, and when he came to Mulan, he asked what she desired most. "I have no need for a minister's post," she said, kneeling before him. "I ask only for a swift camel to carry me back to my hometown." The Khan granted her request, and Mulan rode home with a small escort of her closest comrades. When she arrived at her village, her parents rushed out to embrace her. Her sister put on her finest dress. Her little brother sharpened a knife and slaughtered a pig for a feast. Mulan went into her childhood room, took off the armor she had worn for more than a decade, and dressed herself in the clothes of a woman. She arranged her hair at the window and applied powder to her face. When she walked out to greet her comrades, they stared in shock. "We traveled together for twelve years," one whispered, "and we never knew you were a woman." Mulan smiled. "The buck bounds here and there," she said, "and the doe has narrow eyes. But when two hares run side by side, who can tell the female from the male?"
moral: True honor lies in protecting those you love.

title: The White Snake
summary: A white snake spirit transforms into a beautiful woman and marries a human man. A Buddhist monk discovers her true nature and traps her under Thunder Peak Pagoda.
full_text:
On a stormy day in ancient China, a thousand years ago, a small white snake was captured by a farmer and sold to a wandering herbalist. The herbalist was about to grind the snake into medicine when a young boy named Xu Xian saw the creature's delicate beauty and begged the man to spare it. The herbalist, moved by the boy's compassion, released the snake into the wild. The white snake slithered away into the undergrowth, but it did not forget the kindness of the boy.

For a thousand years, the white snake meditated in the mountains of Emei, absorbing the energy of the moon and the stars. She cultivated her spirit and developed magical powers. When she had finally achieved the ability to transform into human form, she emerged as a woman of breathtaking beauty, with skin like jade, eyes like dark pools, and hair like flowing silk. She took the name Bai Suzhen, which means "White Snake Pure and True."

Bai Suzhen descended from the mountains to seek out Xu Xian, who after a thousand reincarnations, still lived, a gentle herbalist in the city of Hangzhou. She arrived during the Qingming Festival, when the willows were green and the West Lake was covered with boats. She found Xu Xian on Broken Bridge, and as she approached, the heavens opened in a sudden downpour. Xu Xian offered her his umbrella, and they walked together through the rain.

They fell deeply in love. Xu Xian did not know that Bai Suzhen was a snake spirit, and she kept her true nature secret, fearing she would lose him. They were married in a grand ceremony, and Bai Suzhen used her magic to establish a prosperous medicine shop called Baohetang. She proved to be a skilled physician, curing diseases that no other doctor could treat. She gave free medicine to the poor and brought healing to countless people.

But their happiness was not to last. A Buddhist monk named Fahai, the abbot of Jinshan Temple, sensed the presence of a powerful spirit in Hangzhou. He was a zealous exorcist who believed that all supernatural beings were evil and must be destroyed. He investigated and discovered that Bai Suzhen was a white snake spirit.

On the fifth day of the fifth month, the Duanwu Festival, Fahai tricked Xu Xian into giving his wife realgar wine, a potent substance that could force spirits to reveal their true forms. Bai Suzhen, unable to resist the power of the wine, transformed into a giant white snake in front of her husband. Xu Xian, seeing his wife as a serpent, screamed and fell to the ground, dead from shock.

When Bai Suzhen regained her human form and found her husband dead, her grief was inconsolable. She flew to Mount Kunlun, where the immortal herbs grew, guarded by the White Crane Boy and the Deer Girl. She fought her way past the guardians, nearly losing her life, but she seized the magical herb Lingzhi, the mushroom of immortality. She returned home and revived Xu Xian with the herb.

Xu Xian awoke, but the memory of what he had seen haunted him. Fahai took advantage of his fear, convincing him that his wife was a demon who must be exorcised. Fahai kidnapped Xu Xian and held him at Jinshan Temple, refusing to release him until Bai Suzhen left the city forever.

Bai Suzhen begged Fahai to release her husband, but the monk was unmoved. Desperate, she called upon the powers of the sea. She summoned the Dragon Kings of the Four Seas, and with her sister spirit Xiaoqing, a green snake, she raised the waters. A great flood surged against the walls of Jinshan Temple, crashing like the fury of the ocean itself. The waters rose higher and higher, threatening to drown the entire city.

But Fahai was too powerful. He lifted his golden monastic robe, and it expanded like a great canopy, blocking the flood. He threw his alms bowl into the air, and it grew to enormous size, trapping Bai Suzhen in its golden light.

"You have caused enough chaos," Fahai declared. "You will be imprisoned beneath Thunder Peak Pagoda for eternity."

As the pagoda fell upon her, Bai Suzhen saw Xu Xian running toward her, finally free from Fahai's influence, his face streaked with tears. "I do not care what you are!" he cried. "You are my wife, and I love you!"

But it was too late. The pagoda sealed Bai Suzhen deep beneath the earth. Xu Xian became a monk and lived at the foot of the pagoda, tending the grounds and waiting for the day when his wife might be freed.

Years later, their son, Xu Shilin, grew up and passed the imperial examinations, becoming a high official. He returned to Thunder Peak Pagoda and paid homage to his mother, his tears falling on the ancient stones. Moved by his filial piety, the heavens granted Bai Suzhen her freedom. The pagoda crumbled, and she emerged, restored to her human form, to embrace her son and her aging husband.

But some say that Bai Suzhen is still trapped beneath the pagoda, waiting for true love and compassion to finally release her. The story of the White Snake is one of China's Four Great Folktales, a tragic romance about love that transcends the boundaries between human and spirit, mortal and immortal. It reminds us that love, when pure and selfless, can overcome even the greatest obstacles, but that prejudice and fear can destroy the most beautiful of bonds.
moral: Love between different worlds is beautiful but fragile - the white snake's story is a tragedy of prejudice and devotion.
source: Chinese folklore - Feng Menglong's Stories to Caution the World (1624)

title: Hou Yi and the Arrows
summary: Before there were emperors or dynasties, before history was written in bronze and bone, the world was ruled by gods, and among those gods were the ten suns.
full_text: Before there were emperors or dynasties, before history was written in bronze and bone, the world was ruled by gods, and among those gods were the ten suns. They were the children of the Jade Emperor and the goddess Xihe, beings of pure flame who lived in a giant mulberry tree at the edge of the eastern sea. Each morning, one sun would climb into the sky, travel west across the heavens, and descend into the branches of another mulberry tree in the west, while the other nine rested in the eastern tree. This was the natural order, and for millennia, it worked perfectly. But the ten suns grew bored with their solitary journeys. They wanted to experience the world together. So one day, all ten climbed into the sky at once. The effect was immediate and catastrophic. The earth began to char. Rivers boiled away to nothing. The forests of the east burst into flames, and the deserts of the west turned to glass. In the north, the ice caps melted and flooded the plains. In the south, the jungles dried up and turned to tinder. Crops withered in the fields, and the people of China, the children of Yan and Huang, began to die. The great archer Hou Yi looked up at the ten blazing suns and felt a cold fury rise in his chest. He was a hunter of unmatched skill, born of divine lineage, whose arrows could split a falling leaf at a thousand paces. He went to the Jade Emperor and volunteered to stop the suns. The Emperor, who had lost control of his children, gave his reluctant permission. Hou Yi climbed to the top of the Kunlun Mountains, the highest peak in the world, where the air is thin and the sky seems close enough to touch. He carried a bow made from the horn of a celestial ram and arrows fletched with the feathers of the roc. He planted his feet on a granite outcrop, drew a deep breath, and nocked his first arrow. "Suns of heaven," he called, his voice carrying on the wind, "return to your tree, and I will spare you!" The suns laughed. Their combined heat blistered Hou Yi's skin, but he did not flinch. He drew the bowstring to his cheek, aimed at the nearest sun, and released. The arrow flew straight and true. It pierced the sun's flaming heart, and the sun fell from the sky, trailing fire and smoke, crashing into the eastern sea with a hiss that sent steam billowing across the land. It became a three-legged crow, the sacred sun-bird, and its fire was extinguished forever. Hou Yi nocked a second arrow. A second sun fell. Then a third, a fourth, a fifth. The sky darkened as the suns plummeted one by one. The people on earth looked up through tearing eyes and saw the great archer standing on the mountain peak, a black silhouette against the blinding light, shooting down the gods themselves. On the ninth arrow, the Emperor Yao, who ruled the mortal realm, came running up the mountain. "Stop!" he cried, grabbing Hou Yi's arm. "Leave one sun, or the world will be plunged into eternal darkness, and we will all freeze!" Hou Yi lowered his bow. He had shot down nine suns. The tenth, terrified beyond measure, fled to the far edge of the sky and hid behind a cave. The world was plunged into cold and darkness. The surviving sun ignored all pleas to return. Only when the rooster climbed to the highest rooftop and crowed "Brother! Brother!" did the sun peer out from its hiding place. It has been rising every morning since, at the rooster's call, too frightened ever to stay away. For his deeds, the Queen Mother of the West rewarded Hou Yi with an elixir of immortality. But Hou Yi did not drink it. He gave it to his wife, Chang Er, for safekeeping, unwilling to gain immortality without her. This decision would lead to the most famous tragedy in Chinese mythology, but that is another story. For his heroism, Hou Yi is remembered as the greatest archer who ever lived, the savior of a world that was burning, the man who dared to shoot down the sun.
moral: Great power must be matched by great wisdom.

title: Chang Er on the Moon
summary: A very long time ago, in the age when gods still walked among mortals, there were ten suns in the sky.
full_text: A very long time ago, in the age when gods still walked among mortals, there were ten suns in the sky. They were the sons of the Jade Emperor, and each morning one of them was meant to rise in turn, lighting the world for a single day before giving way to the next. But these ten fiery children grew bored with their orderly routine. One morning, all ten rose together, and their combined heat was unbearable. The rivers boiled, the crops withered, the forests burst into flame, and the people of the earth cried out in agony. The Jade Emperor could not control his sons, so he summoned the greatest archer in all the realms. His name was Hou Yi, a hunter of such skill that he could split a willow leaf at a thousand paces. "Go down to earth," the Emperor commanded, "and teach my unruly sons a lesson." Hou Yi descended from heaven with his wife Chang Er at his side. She was a woman of extraordinary beauty, with skin like moonlight and hair like a cascade of black silk, and her heart was as kind as her face was lovely. Together they stood on the scorched earth and looked up at the ten blazing suns. Hou Yi drew his bow, an enormous weapon made from the horn of a celestial ram and the sinew of a dragon. He nocked an arrow and took aim. "Leave now," he shouted to the suns, "and I will spare you!" The suns only laughed, their heat intensifying. Hou Yi released his first arrow. It flew straight and true, piercing the heart of one sun, which fell from the sky as a ball of dying fire and turned into a three-legged crow on the way down. Nine more arrows followed in rapid succession. Nine more suns fell. Hou Yi was about to shoot the last one when the emperor Yao, seeing that the world would be plunged into eternal darkness, snatched the final arrow from his hand. "Leave one sun to light the world," Yao pleaded. Hou Yi lowered his bow. The remaining sun, terrified, fled to the far edge of the sky and hid. It would not return until the rooster crowed and promised it was safe. The people hailed Hou Yi as their savior, but the Jade Emperor was furious. Hou Yi had killed nine of his grandsons. As punishment, the Emperor stripped Hou Yi and Chang Er of their immortality and banished them to live as mortals on earth. Chang Er tried to bear her new life with grace, but she grieved for the immortality she had lost. Every morning she examined her face in the polished bronze mirror, searching for wrinkles, for the first signs of aging. Every night she lay awake, listening to the mortal heartbeat that would one day fall silent. Hou Yi could not bear to see her suffer. He traveled to the Kunlun Mountains, the most sacred peak in the world, and climbed to the palace of the Queen Mother of the West. He begged her for the elixir of immortality. The Queen Mother was moved by his devotion. She gave him a single vial of the precious potion, enough for one person to regain immortality. "Do not take it lightly," she warned. "Once consumed, there is no return." Hou Yi brought the vial home and hid it in the rafters. He did not want to take it alone and leave Chang Er behind, nor did he want her to take it and leave him. He put off the decision, day after day. One afternoon, while Hou Yi was hunting, his apprentice Feng Meng broke into the house. He had seen the vial and desired it for himself. Chang Er, returning early, found him with the elixir in his hands. "Give it to me," Feng Meng demanded, drawing his sword. Chang Er knew she could not let him have it. In desperation, she snatched the vial and drank the entire elixir in one swallow. Immediately, her body grew light as a dandelion seed. She began to rise, floating upward through the roof, past the treetops, into the clouds. Hou Yi returned home to find his wife ascending into the sky. He grabbed his bow and aimed, his fingers trembling on the string. But he could not shoot. She was his beloved. He let the bow fall. Chang Er floated higher and higher, until she reached the moon, the nearest celestial body to earth. There she found a cold, silent palace of jade and silver, and a little jade rabbit who spent eternity pounding the elixir of immortality in a mortar. She has lived there ever since, gazing down at the world she left behind. Every year on the fifteenth night of the eighth lunar month, when the moon is fullest and brightest, Hou Yi would set out her favorite foods and cakes. The people of China do the same to this day, celebrating the Mid-Autumn Festival, remembering Chang Er's sacrifice and eternal longing.
moral: Choices have consequences that echo through eternity.

title: The Monkey King
summary: At the beginning of time, on the Mountain of Flowers and Fruit, which stood at the edge of the Eastern Continent, there was a magic boulder.
full_text: At the beginning of time, on the Mountain of Flowers and Fruit, which stood at the edge of the Eastern Continent, there was a magic boulder. It was thirty-six feet high and twenty-four feet in circumference, and it had been absorbing the essence of heaven and the nurture of earth for countless ages. The sun's yang energy and the moon's yin energy mingled within it, until one day the stone split open with a thunderous crack and produced a stone egg. The wind eroded the egg into the shape of a monkey, and the monkey opened its eyes. Golden beams of light shot from its pupils, piercing the clouds and startling the Jade Emperor in his celestial palace far above. The emperor looked down and saw a monkey bowing to the four cardinal directions. He dismissed it as a curiosity, but he was wrong to do so. This monkey would one day shake heaven to its foundations. The monkey fell in with a troop of forest monkeys and soon proved his courage by leaping through a great waterfall to discover a hidden cave behind it. The cave was furnished with stone tables, stone chairs, and stone cups, and it was large enough to house thousands. The grateful monkeys named him their king. For three hundred years, the Monkey King ruled his troop in peace. But one day, he watched an old monkey die and was seized by the fear of mortality. "If we all must die," he said, "then all our happiness is nothing." He built a raft and set sail across the sea, determined to find the secret of immortality. After many years of searching, he found the sage Patriarch Subodhi, who taught him the seventy-two methods of earthly transformation, allowing him to change his shape and size at will. He learned cloud-somersaulting, a technique that let him travel thirty-three thousand miles in a single leap. He learned spells to call spirits, part water and fire, create illusions, and make himself invulnerable. Most importantly, he learned the way of immortality itself. The Patriarch expelled him for showing off, but the Monkey King hardly cared. He returned to his mountain and established a kingdom of demons and beasts. The Dragon King of the Eastern Sea presented him with a magical staff, the As-You-Will Gold-Banded Cudgel, which could shrink to the size of a needle or grow to the thickness of a millstone. With this weapon, the Monkey King became invincible. News of his power reached heaven, and the Jade Emperor, hoping to control him by appeasement, appointed him Protector of the Horses. When the Monkey King discovered the lowliness of his position, he rebelled. He defeated every army heaven sent against him, stole and ate the peaches of immortality from the celestial orchard, and declared himself the Great Sage Equal to Heaven. The Jade Emperor was forced to call upon the Buddha himself. The Buddha found the Monkey King at the edge of the universe. "If you can jump out of my hand," the Buddha said calmly, "I will abdicate heaven's throne to you." The Monkey King laughed. He somersaulted to the farthest reaches of the cosmos, where he found five pink pillars. Thinking he had reached the edge of existence, he marked one pillar with his name and urinated on another, then returned. "Now give me the throne!" he demanded. "Look down," the Buddha said. There, on the middle finger of the Buddha's hand, was the name the Monkey King had written. He had never left the Buddha's palm at all. Before the Monkey King could protest, the Buddha pressed his hand down, transforming it into the Five Elements Mountain, which pinned the Monkey King to the earth. A talisman was placed on the peak, and there he remained for five hundred years. He ate molten copper and drank iron water. The grass grew over him and trees sprouted through the bones of his wings, but he could not move. Five hundred years passed. A Buddhist monk named Tang Sanzang, on his way to retrieve sacred scriptures from India, came upon the mountain. He climbed to the top, removed the talisman, and freed the Monkey King. In exchange for his freedom, Sun Wukong became the monk's disciple, bound by a golden circlet that tightened around his head whenever he misbehaved. And so began the great journey to the West.
moral: True freedom comes from mastering the self.
